2.play: FLAVIUS: Hence! home, you idle creatures get you home:

3.play: Is this a holiday? what! know you not,

4.play: Being mechanical, you ought not walk

5.play: Upon a labouring day without the sign

6.play: Of your profession? Speak, what trade art thou?

7.play: MARULLUS: Where is thy leather apron and thy rule?

8.play: What dost thou with thy best apparel on?

9.play: You, sir, what trade are you?

10.play: But what trade art thou? answer me directly.

11.play: What trade, thou knave? thou naughty knave, what trade?

12.play: What meanest thou by that? mend me, thou saucy fellow!

13.play: FLAVIUS: Thou art a cobbler, art thou?

14.play: But wherefore art not in thy shop today?

15.play: Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?

16.play: MARULLUS: Wherefore rejoice? What conquest brings he home?

17.play: What tributaries follow him to Rome,

18.play: To grace in captive bonds his chariot-wheels?

19.play: You blocks, you stones, you worse than senseless things!

20.play: O you hard hearts, you cruel men of Rome,

21.play: Knew you not Pompey? Many a time and oft

22.play: Have you climb'd up to walls and battlements,

23.play: To towers and windows, yea, to chimney-tops,

24.play: Your infants in your arms, and there have sat

25.play: The livelong day, with patient expectation,

26.play: To see great Pompey pass the streets of Rome:

27.play: And when you saw his chariot but appear,

28.play: Have you not made an universal shout,

29.play: That Tiber trembled underneath her banks,

30.play: To hear the replication of your sounds

32.play: And do you now put on your best attire?

33.play: And do you now cull out a holiday?

34.play: And do you now strew flowers in his way

35.play: That comes in triumph over Pompey's blood? Be gone!

36.play: Run to your houses, fall upon your knees,

37.play: Pray to the gods to intermit the plague

38.play: That needs must light on this ingratitude.

39.play: FLAVIUS: Go, go, good countrymen, and, for this fault,

40.play: Assemble all the poor men of your sort;

41.play: Draw them to Tiber banks, and weep your tears

42.play: Into the channel, till the lowest stream

43.play: Do kiss the most exalted shores of all.

44.play: See whether their basest metal be not moved;

45.play: They vanish tongue-tied in their guiltiness.

46.play: Go you down that way towards the Capitol;

48.play: You know it is the feast of Lupercal.

49.play: FLAVIUS: It is no matter; let no images

50.play: Be hung with Caesar's trophies. I'll about,

51.play: And drive away the vulgar from the streets:

52.play: So do you too, where you perceive them thick.

53.play: These growing feathers pluck'd from Caesar's wing

54.play: Will make him fly an ordinary pitch,

55.play: Who else would soar above the view of men

56.play: And keep us all in servile fearfulness.

1.play: Flourish. Enter CAESAR; ANTONY, for the course;  CALPURNIA, PORTIA, DECIUS BRUTUS, CICERO, BRUTUS,  CASSIUS, and CASCA; a great crowd following, among them a Soothsayer

6.play: CAESAR: Stand you directly in Antonius' way,

7.play: When he doth run his course. Antonius!

9.play: CAESAR: Forget not, in your speed, Antonius,

10.play: To touch Calpurnia; for our elders say,

11.play: The barren, touched in this holy chase,

14.play: When Caesar says 'do this,' it is perform'd.

15.play: CAESAR: Set on; and leave no ceremony out.

17.play: CASCA: Bid every noise be still: peace yet again!

18.play: CAESAR: Who is it in the press that calls on me?

19.play: I hear a tongue, shriller than all the music,

20.play: Cry 'Caesar!' Speak; Caesar is turn'd to hear.

22.play: BRUTUS: A soothsayer bids you beware the ides of March.

23.play: CAESAR: Set him before me; let me see his face.

24.play: CASSIUS: Fellow, come from the throng; look upon Caesar.

25.play: CAESAR: What say'st thou to me now? speak once again.

26.play: He is a dreamer; let us leave him: pass.

27.play: CASSIUS: Will you go see the order of the course?

30.play: BRUTUS: I am not gamesome: I do lack some part

31.play: Of that quick spirit that is in Antony.

32.play: Let me not hinder, Cassius, your desires;

34.play: CASSIUS: Brutus, I do observe you now of late:

35.play: I have not from your eyes that gentleness

36.play: And show of love as I was wont to have:

37.play: You bear too stubborn and too strange a hand

38.play: Over your friend that loves you.

40.play: Be not deceived: if I have veil'd my look,

41.play: I turn the trouble of my countenance

42.play: Merely upon myself. Vexed I am

43.play: Of late with passions of some difference,

45.play: Which give some soil perhaps to my behaviors;

46.play: But let not therefore my good friends be grieved--

47.play: Among which number, Cassius, be you one--

48.play: Nor construe any further my neglect,

49.play: Than that poor Brutus, with himself at war,

50.play: Forgets the shows of love to other men.

51.play: CASSIUS: Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion;

52.play: By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried

53.play: Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations.

54.play: Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?

55.play: BRUTUS: No, Cassius; for the eye sees not itself,

56.play: But by reflection, by some other things.

58.play: And it is very much lamented, Brutus,

59.play: That you have no such mirrors as will turn

60.play: Your hidden worthiness into your eye,

61.play: That you might see your shadow. I have heard,

62.play: Where many of the best respect in Rome,

63.play: Except immortal Caesar, speaking of Brutus

64.play: And groaning underneath this age's yoke,

65.play: Have wish'd that noble Brutus had his eyes.

66.play: BRUTUS: Into what dangers would you lead me, Cassius,

67.play: That you would have me seek into myself

68.play: For that which is not in me?

69.play: CASSIUS: Therefore, good Brutus, be prepared to hear:

70.play: And since you know you cannot see yourself

71.play: So well as by reflection, I, your glass,

73.play: That of yourself which you yet know not of.

74.play: And be not jealous on me, gentle Brutus:

75.play: Were I a common laugher, or did use

76.play: To stale with ordinary oaths my love

77.play: To every new protester; if you know

78.play: That I do fawn on men and hug them hard

79.play: And after scandal them, or if you know

80.play: That I profess myself in banqueting

81.play: To all the rout, then hold me dangerous.

82.play: BRUTUS: What means this shouting? I do fear, the people

84.play: CASSIUS: Ay, do you fear it?

85.play: Then must I think you would not have it so.

86.play: BRUTUS: I would not, Cassius; yet I love him well.

87.play: But wherefore do you hold me here so long?

88.play: What is it that you would impart to me?

89.play: If it be aught toward the general good,

90.play: Set honour in one eye and death i' the other,

91.play: And I will look on both indifferently,

92.play: For let the gods so speed me as I love

93.play: The name of honour more than I fear death.

94.play: CASSIUS: I know that virtue to be in you, Brutus,

95.play: As well as I do know your outward favour.

96.play: Well, honour is the subject of my story.

97.play: I cannot tell what you and other men

98.play: Think of this life; but, for my single self,

99.play: I had as lief not be as live to be

100.play: In awe of such a thing as I myself.

101.play: I was born free as Caesar; so were you:

102.play: We both have fed as well, and we can both

103.play: Endure the winter's cold as well as he:

104.play: For once, upon a raw and gusty day,

105.play: The troubled Tiber chafing with her shores,

106.play: Caesar said to me 'Darest thou, Cassius, now

107.play: Leap in with me into this angry flood,

108.play: And swim to yonder point?' Upon the word,

109.play: Accoutred as I was, I plunged in

110.play: And bade him follow; so indeed he did.

111.play: The torrent roar'd, and we did buffet it

112.play: With lusty sinews, throwing it aside

113.play: And stemming it with hearts of controversy;

114.play: But ere we could arrive the point proposed,

115.play: Caesar cried 'Help me, Cassius, or I sink!'

116.play: I, as Aeneas, our great ancestor,

117.play: Did from the flames of Troy upon his shoulder

118.play: The old Anchises bear, so from the waves of Tiber

119.play: Did I the tired Caesar. And this man

120.play: Is now become a god, and Cassius is

121.play: A wretched creature and must bend his body,

122.play: If Caesar carelessly but nod on him.

123.play: He had a fever when he was in Spain,

124.play: And when the fit was on him, I did mark

125.play: How he did shake: 'tis true, this god did shake;

126.play: His coward lips did from their colour fly,

127.play: And that same eye whose bend doth awe the world

128.play: Did lose his lustre: I did hear him groan:

129.play: Ay, and that tongue of his that bade the Romans

130.play: Mark him and write his speeches in their books,

131.play: Alas, it cried 'Give me some drink, Titinius,'

132.play: As a sick girl. Ye gods, it doth amaze me

133.play: A man of such a feeble temper should

134.play: So get the start of the majestic world

137.play: I do believe that these applauses are

138.play: For some new honours that are heap'd on Caesar.

139.play: CASSIUS: Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world

140.play: Like a Colossus, and we petty men

141.play: Walk under his huge legs and peep about

143.play: Men at some time are masters of their fates:

144.play: The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars,

145.play: But in ourselves, that we are underlings.

146.play: Brutus and Caesar: what should be in that 'Caesar'?

147.play: Why should that name be sounded more than yours?

148.play: Write them together, yours is as fair a name;

149.play: Sound them, it doth become the mouth as well;

150.play: Weigh them, it is as heavy; conjure with 'em,

151.play: Brutus will start a spirit as soon as Caesar.

152.play: Now, in the names of all the gods at once,

153.play: Upon what meat doth this our Caesar feed,

154.play: That he is grown so great? Age, thou art shamed!

155.play: Rome, thou hast lost the breed of noble bloods!

156.play: When went there by an age, since the great flood,

157.play: But it was famed with more than with one man?

158.play: When could they say till now, that talk'd of Rome,

159.play: That her wide walls encompass'd but one man?

160.play: Now is it Rome indeed and room enough,

161.play: When there is in it but one only man.

162.play: O, you and I have heard our fathers say,

163.play: There was a Brutus once that would have brook'd

164.play: The eternal devil to keep his state in Rome

166.play: BRUTUS: That you do love me, I am nothing jealous;

167.play: What you would work me to, I have some aim:

168.play: How I have thought of this and of these times,

169.play: I shall recount hereafter; for this present,

170.play: I would not, so with love I might entreat you,

171.play: Be any further moved. What you have said

172.play: I will consider; what you have to say

173.play: I will with patience hear, and find a time

174.play: Both meet to hear and answer such high things.

175.play: Till then, my noble friend, chew upon this:

176.play: Brutus had rather be a villager

177.play: Than to repute himself a son of Rome

178.play: Under these hard conditions as this time

179.play: Is like to lay upon us.

180.play: CASSIUS: I am glad that my weak words

181.play: Have struck but thus much show of fire from Brutus.

182.play: BRUTUS: The games are done and Caesar is returning.

183.play: CASSIUS: As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve;

184.play: And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you

185.play: What hath proceeded worthy note to-day.

186.play: BRUTUS: I will do so. But, look you, Cassius,

187.play: The angry spot doth glow on Caesar's brow,

188.play: And all the rest look like a chidden train:

189.play: Calpurnia's cheek is pale; and Cicero

190.play: Looks with such ferret and such fiery eyes

191.play: As we have seen him in the Capitol,

192.play: Being cross'd in conference by some senators.

193.play: CASSIUS: Casca will tell us what the matter is.

196.play: CAESAR: Let me have men about me that are fat;

197.play: Sleek-headed men and such as sleep o' nights:

198.play: Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry look;

199.play: He thinks too much: such men are dangerous.

200.play: ANTONY: Fear him not, Caesar; he's not dangerous;

201.play: He is a noble Roman and well given.

202.play: CAESAR: Would he were fatter! But I fear him not:

203.play: Yet if my name were liable to fear,

204.play: I do not know the man I should avoid

205.play: So soon as that spare Cassius. He reads much;

206.play: He is a great observer and he looks

207.play: Quite through the deeds of men: he loves no plays,

208.play: As thou dost, Antony; he hears no music;

209.play: Seldom he smiles, and smiles in such a sort

210.play: As if he mock'd himself and scorn'd his spirit

211.play: That could be moved to smile at any thing.

212.play: Such men as he be never at heart's ease

213.play: Whiles they behold a greater than themselves,

214.play: And therefore are they very dangerous.

215.play: I rather tell thee what is to be fear'd

216.play: Than what I fear; for always I am Caesar.

217.play: Come on my right hand, for this ear is deaf,

218.play: And tell me truly what thou think'st of him.

219.play: CASCA: You pull'd me by the cloak; would you speak with me?

220.play: BRUTUS: Ay, Casca; tell us what hath chanced to-day,

222.play: CASCA: Why, you were with him, were you not?

223.play: BRUTUS: I should not then ask Casca what had chanced.

224.play: CASCA: Why, there was a crown offered him: and being

225.play: offered him, he put it by with the back of his hand,

226.play: thus; and then the people fell a-shouting.

227.play: BRUTUS: What was the second noise for?

229.play: CASSIUS: They shouted thrice: what was the last cry for?

231.play: BRUTUS: Was the crown offered him thrice?

232.play: CASCA: Ay, marry, was't, and he put it by thrice, every

233.play: time gentler than other, and at every putting-by

235.play: CASSIUS: Who offered him the crown?

237.play: BRUTUS: Tell us the manner of it, gentle Casca.

238.play: CASCA: I can as well be hanged as tell the manner of it:

239.play: it was mere foolery; I did not mark it. I saw Mark

240.play: Antony offer him a crown;--yet 'twas not a crown

241.play: neither, 'twas one of these coronets;--and, as I told

242.play: you, he put it by once: but, for all that, to my

243.play: thinking, he would fain have had it. Then he

244.play: offered it to him again; then he put it by again:

245.play: but, to my thinking, he was very loath to lay his

246.play: fingers off it. And then he offered it the third

247.play: time; he put it the third time by: and still as he

248.play: refused it, the rabblement hooted and clapped their

249.play: chapped hands and threw up their sweaty night-caps

250.play: and uttered such a deal of stinking breath because

251.play: Caesar refused the crown that it had almost choked

252.play: Caesar; for he swounded and fell down at it: and

253.play: for mine own part, I durst not laugh, for fear of

254.play: opening my lips and receiving the bad air.

255.play: CASSIUS: But, soft, I pray you: what, did Caesar swound?

256.play: CASCA: He fell down in the market-place, and foamed at

258.play: BRUTUS: 'Tis very like: he hath the failing sickness.

259.play: CASSIUS: No, Caesar hath it not; but you and I,

260.play: And honest Casca, we have the falling sickness.

261.play: CASCA: I know not what you mean by that; but, I am sure,

262.play: Caesar fell down. If the tag-rag people did not

263.play: clap him and hiss him, according as he pleased and

264.play: displeased them, as they use to do the players in

265.play: the theatre, I am no true man.

266.play: BRUTUS: What said he when he came unto himself?

267.play: CASCA: Marry, before he fell down, when he perceived the

268.play: common herd was glad he refused the crown, he

269.play: plucked me ope his doublet and offered them his

270.play: throat to cut. An I had been a man of any

271.play: occupation, if I would not have taken him at a word,

272.play: I would I might go to hell among the rogues. And so

273.play: he fell. When he came to himself again, he said,

274.play: If he had done or said any thing amiss, he desired

275.play: their worships to think it was his infirmity. Three

276.play: or four wenches, where I stood, cried 'Alas, good

277.play: soul!' and forgave him with all their hearts: but

278.play: there's no heed to be taken of them; if Caesar had

279.play: stabbed their mothers, they would have done no less.

280.play: BRUTUS: And after that, he came, thus sad, away?

282.play: CASSIUS: Did Cicero say any thing?

285.play: CASCA: Nay, an I tell you that, Ill ne'er look you i' the

286.play: face again: but those that understood him smiled at

287.play: one another and shook their heads; but, for mine own

288.play: part, it was Greek to me. I could tell you more

289.play: news too: Marullus and Flavius, for pulling scarfs

290.play: off Caesar's images, are put to silence. Fare you

291.play: well. There was more foolery yet, if I could

293.play: CASSIUS: Will you sup with me to-night, Casca?

294.play: CASCA: No, I am promised forth.

295.play: CASSIUS: Will you dine with me to-morrow?

296.play: CASCA: Ay, if I be alive and your mind hold and your dinner

298.play: CASSIUS: Good: I will expect you.

300.play: BRUTUS: What a blunt fellow is this grown to be!

301.play: He was quick mettle when he went to school.

302.play: CASSIUS: So is he now in execution

303.play: Of any bold or noble enterprise,

304.play: However he puts on this tardy form.

305.play: This rudeness is a sauce to his good wit,

306.play: Which gives men stomach to digest his words

308.play: BRUTUS: And so it is. For this time I will leave you:

309.play: To-morrow, if you please to speak with me,

310.play: I will come home to you; or, if you will,

311.play: Come home to me, and I will wait for you.

312.play: CASSIUS: I will do so: till then, think of the world.

313.play: Well, Brutus, thou art noble; yet, I see,

314.play: Thy honourable metal may be wrought

315.play: From that it is disposed: therefore it is meet

316.play: That noble minds keep ever with their likes;

317.play: For who so firm that cannot be seduced?

318.play: Caesar doth bear me hard; but he loves Brutus:

319.play: If I were Brutus now and he were Cassius,

320.play: He should not humour me. I will this night,

321.play: In several hands, in at his windows throw,

322.play: As if they came from several citizens,

323.play: Writings all tending to the great opinion

324.play: That Rome holds of his name; wherein obscurely

325.play: Caesar's ambition shall be glanced at:

326.play: And after this let Caesar seat him sure;

327.play: For we will shake him, or worse days endure.

1.play: CICERO: Good even, Casca: brought you Caesar home?

2.play: Why are you breathless? and why stare you so?

3.play: CASCA: Are not you moved, when all the sway of earth

4.play: Shakes like a thing unfirm? O Cicero,

5.play: I have seen tempests, when the scolding winds

6.play: Have rived the knotty oaks, and I have seen

7.play: The ambitious ocean swell and rage and foam,

8.play: To be exalted with the threatening clouds:

9.play: But never till to-night, never till now,

10.play: Did I go through a tempest dropping fire.

11.play: Either there is a civil strife in heaven,

12.play: Or else the world, too saucy with the gods,

14.play: CICERO: Why, saw you any thing more wonderful?

15.play: CASCA: A common slave--you know him well by sight--

16.play: Held up his left hand, which did flame and burn

17.play: Like twenty torches join'd, and yet his hand,

18.play: Not sensible of fire, remain'd unscorch'd.

19.play: Besides--I ha' not since put up my sword--

20.play: Against the Capitol I met a lion,

21.play: Who glared upon me, and went surly by,

22.play: Without annoying me: and there were drawn

23.play: Upon a heap a hundred ghastly women,

24.play: Transformed with their fear; who swore they saw

25.play: Men all in fire walk up and down the streets.

26.play: And yesterday the bird of night did sit

27.play: Even at noon-day upon the market-place,

28.play: Hooting and shrieking. When these prodigies

29.play: Do so conjointly meet, let not men say

30.play: 'These are their reasons; they are natural;'

31.play: For, I believe, they are portentous things

32.play: Unto the climate that they point upon.

33.play: CICERO: Indeed, it is a strange-disposed time:

34.play: But men may construe things after their fashion,

35.play: Clean from the purpose of the things themselves.

36.play: Come Caesar to the Capitol to-morrow?

37.play: CASCA: He doth; for he did bid Antonius

38.play: Send word to you he would be there to-morrow.

39.play: CICERO: Good night then, Casca: this disturbed sky

45.play: CASCA: Your ear is good. Cassius, what night is this!

46.play: CASSIUS: A very pleasing night to honest men.

47.play: CASCA: Who ever knew the heavens menace so?

48.play: CASSIUS: Those that have known the earth so full of faults.

49.play: For my part, I have walk'd about the streets,

50.play: Submitting me unto the perilous night,

51.play: And, thus unbraced, Casca, as you see,

52.play: Have bared my bosom to the thunder-stone;

53.play: And when the cross blue lightning seem'd to open

54.play: The breast of heaven, I did present myself

55.play: Even in the aim and very flash of it.

56.play: CASCA: But wherefore did you so much tempt the heavens?

57.play: It is the part of men to fear and tremble,

58.play: When the most mighty gods by tokens send

59.play: Such dreadful heralds to astonish us.

60.play: CASSIUS: You are dull, Casca, and those sparks of life

61.play: That should be in a Roman you do want,

62.play: Or else you use not. You look pale and gaze

63.play: And put on fear and cast yourself in wonder,

64.play: To see the strange impatience of the heavens:

65.play: But if you would consider the true cause

66.play: Why all these fires, why all these gliding ghosts,

67.play: Why birds and beasts from quality and kind,

68.play: Why old men fool and children calculate,

69.play: Why all these things change from their ordinance

71.play: To monstrous quality,--why, you shall find

72.play: That heaven hath infused them with these spirits,

73.play: To make them instruments of fear and warning

75.play: Now could I, Casca, name to thee a man

77.play: That thunders, lightens, opens graves, and roars

78.play: As doth the lion in the Capitol,

79.play: A man no mightier than thyself or me

80.play: In personal action, yet prodigious grown

81.play: And fearful, as these strange eruptions are.

82.play: CASCA: 'Tis Caesar that you mean; is it not, Cassius?

83.play: CASSIUS: Let it be who it is: for Romans now

84.play: Have thews and limbs like to their ancestors;

85.play: But, woe the while! our fathers' minds are dead,

86.play: And we are govern'd with our mothers' spirits;

87.play: Our yoke and sufferance show us womanish.

88.play: CASCA: Indeed, they say the senators tomorrow

89.play: Mean to establish Caesar as a king;

90.play: And he shall wear his crown by sea and land,

91.play: In every place, save here in Italy.

92.play: CASSIUS: I know where I will wear this dagger then;

93.play: Cassius from bondage will deliver Cassius:

94.play: Therein, ye gods, you make the weak most strong;

95.play: Therein, ye gods, you tyrants do defeat:

96.play: Nor stony tower, nor walls of beaten brass,

97.play: Nor airless dungeon, nor strong links of iron,

98.play: Can be retentive to the strength of spirit;

99.play: But life, being weary of these worldly bars,

100.play: Never lacks power to dismiss itself.

101.play: If I know this, know all the world besides,

102.play: That part of tyranny that I do bear

103.play: I can shake off at pleasure.

105.play: So every bondman in his own hand bears

106.play: The power to cancel his captivity.

107.play: CASSIUS: And why should Caesar be a tyrant then?

108.play: Poor man! I know he would not be a wolf,

109.play: But that he sees the Romans are but sheep:

110.play: He were no lion, were not Romans hinds.

111.play: Those that with haste will make a mighty fire

112.play: Begin it with weak straws: what trash is Rome,

113.play: What rubbish and what offal, when it serves

114.play: For the base matter to illuminate

115.play: So vile a thing as Caesar! But, O grief,
